_purpose: Local economy timeline for the MA Eastern Colonial cluster (Plymouth, Norfolk, Bristol, Barnstable, Essex, Suffolk). Covers 1620-1960 across seven economic eras — from Plymouth Colony subsistence through maritime supremacy, industrial revolution, immigrant labor economies, and the postwar suburban transformation.
display_name: MA Eastern Colonial — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Subsistence farming, fishing, lumber, and fur trade. Coastal communities developed cod fisheries and West Indies trade.
economic_eras.description: The earliest economy was survival. Plymouth Colony's first decade was marked by starvation and dependence on Wampanoag corn cultivation techniques. By the 1640s, mixed farming (corn, rye, oats, cattle, swine) established a subsistence base, but the thin, glaciated New England soils never produced agricultural surpluses comparable to the Chesapeake or the Connecticut Valley. The sea filled the gap. By 1660, the cod fishery was the economic engine of the Massachusetts coast — salt cod shipped to the Catholic markets of Spain, Portugal, and the West Indies, with the lowest-grade fish feeding enslaved workers on Caribbean sugar plantations. The triangular trade (fish and lumber south, sugar and molasses north, rum and manufactured goods to Africa and back) connected even small fishing ports to global commerce. Shipbuilding emerged along the North River (Plymouth/Norfolk boundary) and in Salem, Newbury, and Gloucester.

county_specifics.note: Plymouth Colony's subsistence farming supplemented by fishing and the North River shipbuilding industry. Plymouth Harbor served as a modest fishing port.
Boston dominated from the start — the deepest harbor, the seat of government, and the commercial capital. Long Wharf (1710) extended into the harbor to accommodate deep-draft vessels.
Salem, Gloucester, and Marblehead became the leading fishing ports. Salem developed the West Indies trade. Ipswich and Newbury were agricultural towns with fishing supplements.

Cape Cod towns combined subsistence farming on poor soil with shore fishing, salt making, and whaling from small boats (shore whaling). Provincetown was a seasonal fishing camp before becoming a permanent settlement.
Taunton was an early iron-smelting center using bog iron. Rehoboth and Dartmouth (later New Bedford) were agricultural and livestock communities.
Not yet a separate county (part of Suffolk until 1793). Dedham, Braintree, and Weymouth were farming communities within the Boston economic orbit.

key_infrastructure.name: Cod fishery — Grand Banks and inshore
key_infrastructure.impact: Salt cod became the economic foundation of coastal Massachusetts. Fish shipped to the West Indies and Mediterranean financed the importation of manufactured goods from England.

key_infrastructure.name: North River shipyards
key_infrastructure.impact: Over 1,000 vessels were built on the North River between 1678 and 1871. Shipbuilding provided employment and maritime capital to Plymouth and Norfolk County communities.

key_infrastructure.name: Boston Long Wharf
key_infrastructure.impact: Extended 1/3 mile into the harbor, allowing the largest merchant ships to dock directly. Established Boston as the commercial hub of New England.

major_employers.name: Family farms (subsistence)
major_employers.employment_scale: Universal — every family farmed; supplemental trades (fishing, shipbuilding, blacksmithing) provided cash or barter income

wage_benchmarks.source: Vickers, Daniel. Farmers and Fishermen. UNC Press, 1994, ch. 3.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Massachusetts in {year}, the colony offered two things: thin soil and deep water. The farm kept the family alive; the cod fishery — salting, packing, and shipping fish to the Catholic countries of Europe — was what connected {ancestor_name}'s village to the wider world.
economic_eras.source: Vickers, Daniel. Farmers and Fishermen: Two Centuries of Work in Essex County, Massachusetts, 1630-1850. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1994.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Deep-sea fishing, whaling, merchant shipping (West Indies, China, East Indies trade), shipbuilding, rum distilling
economic_eras.description: Massachusetts achieved maritime supremacy in the eighteenth century. Salem became the sixth largest city in America by 1790, powered by the East India and China trade. New Bedford and Nantucket dominated whaling. Gloucester and Marblehead were the fishing capitals. Boston was the commercial and financial center, handling insurance, banking, and wholesale distribution for the entire region. The economy was global: Massachusetts ships carried cod to Bilbao, rum to Africa, sugar from the West Indies, silk from Canton, and pepper from Sumatra. The Revolution disrupted maritime commerce but the post-war period (1783-1807) saw Salem's greatest prosperity. The Embargo Act (1807) and the War of 1812 devastated the maritime economy, pushing capital toward manufacturing.

county_specifics.note: Salem was the commercial capital of the East India and China trade. Marblehead was the leading fishing port (over 1,000 men at sea annually). Newburyport built merchant ships.
Boston served as the financial and insurance center for the maritime economy. State Street counting houses handled the capital that financed voyages.
New Bedford began its rise as a whaling port, surpassing Nantucket by the 1820s. Taunton iron works supplied ship hardware.
Cape Cod men crewed whaling and fishing vessels from every port. Wellfleet oysters were a cash crop. Salt works expanded.
North River shipyards reached peak production. Duxbury and Scituate launched merchant vessels. Plymouth town remained modest.
Quincy granite quarrying began (first major quarry 1826). Braintree and Weymouth supplied agricultural products to Boston.

key_infrastructure.name: Salem Custom House (federal)
key_infrastructure.impact: Salem was designated a port of entry. Custom duties collected in Salem were a significant source of federal revenue in the early republic.

key_infrastructure.name: Granite Railway (first commercial railroad in US)
key_infrastructure.impact: Built to transport Quincy granite to the Neponset River for shipment. Pioneered railroad technology that would transform the continent.

key_infrastructure.name: Whaling fleet expansion (New Bedford)
key_infrastructure.impact: New Bedford's deep harbor attracted the whaling industry from Nantucket. By 1815, New Bedford had overtaken Nantucket as the whaling capital.

major_employers.name: Merchant shipping firms (Salem, Boston)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of sailors, stevedores, chandlers, ropemakers, and support trades

major_employers.name: Whaling firms (New Bedford, Nantucket)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of ships, thousands of crew. Voyage crews of 25-35 men each.

major_employers.name: Fishing fleet (Gloucester, Marblehead)
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 1,000 men from Marblehead alone at peak.

wage_benchmarks.source: Dolin, Eric Jay. Leviathan. W.W. Norton, 2007, ch. 8.
Bridenbaugh, Carl. The Colonial Craftsman. University of Chicago Press, 1950.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name}'s livelihood depended on the sea — whether {pronoun_subject} sailed it, built the ships that crossed it, or sold the goods that came back on them. Massachusetts was a maritime civilization, and the distance between {ancestor_name}'s doorstep and Canton or Sumatra was measured in months, not miles.
economic_eras.source: Vickers, Farmers and Fishermen. Dolin, Eric Jay. Leviathan: The History of Whaling in America. New York: W.W. Norton, 2007.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Textile manufacturing, shoe manufacturing, whaling at peak, railroad construction, granite quarrying
economic_eras.description: The War of 1812 drove maritime capital into manufacturing. Boston investors built the first power looms at Waltham (1814) and then created the planned mill city of Lowell (1823). The industrial model spread: Lawrence on the Merrimack (1845), Fall River on the Quequechan (1811+, expanding rapidly by 1840), and New Bedford transitioned from whaling to textiles. Shoe manufacturing mechanized in Lynn, Brockton, and Haverhill. Quincy granite quarries supplied building stone for the Bunker Hill Monument, US Custom Houses, and public buildings across the Eastern seaboard. Whaling reached its peak in the 1850s — New Bedford was, per capita, the wealthiest city in America. The entire economy ran on immigrant labor: Irish Famine refugees (1845-1855) provided the mill workforce, replacing the Yankee mill girls who had staffed the first generation of factories.

county_specifics.note: Lawrence built as a planned mill city (1845). Lynn and Haverhill became shoe manufacturing centers. Salem declined as a maritime port, shifted to leather and textile trades.
Fall River became the largest cotton textile center in America. New Bedford's whaling fleet peaked at over 300 vessels. Taunton developed as a manufacturing center (silver, stoves, machinery).
Quincy granite quarries employed hundreds. Brockton shoe manufacturing began. Northern Norfolk towns began suburban development along the railroad.
North River shipbuilding declined as iron ships replaced wood. Brockton (partly in Plymouth County) shoe factories expanded. Cranberry cultivation commercialized.
Boston became the financial center of New England industry. Banking, insurance, and wholesale distribution concentrated on State Street. Irish immigration transformed the city's demographics.
Cape Cod's economy peaked and began declining — whaling moved to New Bedford, salt works closed, fishing remained but the population drained away to mainland cities.

key_infrastructure.name: Boston and Worcester Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: First major railroad west from Boston. Connected the coast to the interior and began the transformation from maritime to rail-based commerce.

key_infrastructure.name: Old Colony Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Boston to Plymouth, Fall River, and New Bedford. Made commuter suburbs possible in Norfolk and Plymouth counties.

key_infrastructure.name: Lawrence dam and canal system
key_infrastructure.impact: The Essex Company dammed the Merrimack to create waterpower for a planned industrial city. Lawrence grew from nothing to 18,000 people in fifteen years.

key_infrastructure.name: Petroleum discovery (Pennsylvania)
key_infrastructure.impact: Petroleum began replacing whale oil for lighting, initiating the long decline of New Bedford's whaling industry.

major_employers.name: Fall River textile mills
major_employers.employment_scale: Over 5,000 workers by 1860, growing to 30,000+ by 1880

major_employers.name: New Bedford whaling fleet
major_employers.employment_scale: 10,000+ men at sea annually at peak; thousands more in shore support

major_employers.name: Shoe factories (Lynn, Brockton, Haverhill)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands; Lynn alone produced 4 million pairs annually by 1860

wage_benchmarks.source: Dublin, Thomas. Women at Work: The Transformation of Work and Community in Lowell, Massachusetts, 1826-1860. Columbia University Press, 1979.
Cumbler, John T. Reasonable Use. Oxford University Press, 2001.
Hurd, Hamilton. History of Norfolk County. J.W. Lewis, 1884.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By {year}, the old maritime economy that had sustained {ancestor_name}'s parents was giving way to something new — the mills needed hands, the shoe factories needed stitchers, and the immigrant ships were delivering both. {ancestor_name} could work a loom in Lawrence or stitch uppers in Lynn for wages that came every week, not every three years.
economic_eras.source: Dublin, Thomas. Women at Work. Columbia University Press, 1979. Cumbler, Reasonable Use. Dolin, Leviathan.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Textile manufacturing, shoe manufacturing, fishing, granite quarrying, shipbuilding (steel). Massive immigration — Irish, French-Canadian, Italian, Portuguese, Eastern European — supplied labor.
economic_eras.description: Eastern Massachusetts reached its industrial peak between the Civil War and World War I. Fall River became the cotton textile capital of America with over 100 mills. Lawrence was second. Lynn and Brockton produced shoes by the millions. Quincy's Fore River Shipyard built battleships. Gloucester remained the fishing capital. New Bedford transitioned from whaling to fine-count cotton textiles. The workforce was overwhelmingly immigrant: Irish (dominant by the 1850s), then French-Canadians (1870s-1890s mill towns), then Italians (1890s-1910s), then Portuguese (1890s-1920s, especially New Bedford and Fall River), then Eastern Europeans (Poles, Lithuanians, Syrians in Lawrence). Each wave filled the mills, quarries, and factories, forming ethnic neighborhoods, parishes, and mutual aid societies that structured daily life. Labor conflict was intense — the Lynn shoe strike (1860), the Lawrence Bread and Roses strike (1912), and the New Bedford textile strike (1928) were nationally significant.

county_specifics.note: Fall River peaked at 120+ textile mills. New Bedford transitioned to textiles after whaling declined. Both cities drew massive Portuguese immigration. Taunton diversified into silver, stoves, and machinery.

Lawrence was the most ethnically diverse city in America by 1910 — over 25 nationalities. The 1912 Bread and Roses strike made national headlines. Salem shifted to leather manufacturing. Gloucester fishing fleet remained strong.
Quincy's Fore River Shipyard (1884) built warships. Brockton's shoe industry peaked. Northern towns became commuter suburbs.
Brockton shoe manufacturing peaked (63,000 population by 1910). Cranberry industry expanded with Cape Verdean and Finnish labor.
Boston's economy diversified — banking, insurance, education, publishing, and port operations. Immigration transformed the city's politics (Irish machine politics).
Population declined as young people left for mainland jobs. Portuguese fishing community in Provincetown grew. Tourism began.

key_infrastructure.name: Fore River Shipyard (Quincy)
key_infrastructure.impact: Built warships for the US Navy — battleships, destroyers, and later aircraft carriers. Employed up to 32,000 workers during WWII.

key_infrastructure.name: Electric streetcar systems
key_infrastructure.impact: Streetcar lines connected mill towns to surrounding communities and enabled the first wave of suburban commuting in Norfolk and Essex counties.

key_infrastructure.name: Cape Cod Canal
key_infrastructure.impact: Eliminated the dangerous passage around Cape Cod. Transformed the Cape from a peninsula to an island-like destination.

major_employers.name: Shoe factories (Lynn, Brockton, Haverhill)
major_employers.employment_scale: Tens of thousands across the three cities

major_employers.name: Fore River Shipyard
major_employers.employment_scale: 3,000-5,000 workers peacetime; up to 32,000 during wartime

major_employers.name: Gloucester fishing fleet
major_employers.employment_scale: Several thousand men at sea; hundreds in shore processing

wage_benchmarks.source: Silvia, Philip T. Victorian Vistas. R.E. Smith Printing, 1987.
Quincy Historical Society. Fore River Shipyard.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in {county} County from {origin_county} around {year}, the mills and factories were hiring anyone who could stand at a loom or work a stitching machine — and the neighborhoods were already filling with people who spoke the same language {pronoun_subject} did.
economic_eras.source: Silvia, Victorian Vistas. Watson, Bruce. Bread and Roses. Viking, 2005. Cumbler, Reasonable Use.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Textile collapse (mills moving south), shoe manufacturing declining, fishing sustaining, defense industry beginning, Depression unemployment
economic_eras.description: The 1920s and 1930s devastated the industrial economy. Textile mills relocated to the Carolinas for cheaper, non-union labor — Fall River lost half its textile employment by 1935. New Bedford's 1928 textile strike accelerated departures. Shoe manufacturing declined as production moved to cheaper-labor regions. The Great Depression hit mill towns with particular severity — unemployment in Fall River and Lawrence exceeded 30%. The fishing industry sustained Gloucester and New Bedford's Portuguese community but offered no growth. The only bright spots were the Fore River Shipyard (naval construction) and the beginnings of Route 128 development in Norfolk County.

county_specifics.note: Fall River and New Bedford were devastated. Entire mill blocks stood empty. The Portuguese fishing fleet at New Bedford was the one surviving industry.
Lawrence lost textile jobs to the South. Salem's leather industry declined. Gloucester fishing remained viable but couldn't absorb the unemployed mill workforce.
Less affected than mill counties. The Fore River Shipyard provided defense work. Suburban development continued in towns closest to Boston.
Brockton's shoe industry declined. Cranberry prices collapsed during the Depression. Rural Plymouth County remained poor.
Boston's diversified economy was more resilient than the mill towns. Financial services, education, and government employment provided stability.
The Cape's tourism economy was hit by the Depression but less severely than manufacturing. Fishing continued. The WPA employed workers on beach and road projects.

key_infrastructure.name: Route 128 (circumferential highway)
key_infrastructure.impact: The highway that would become 'America's Technology Highway' was planned during this era, eventually transforming Norfolk County's economy.

major_employers.name: Remaining textile mills (declining)
major_employers.employment_scale: Declining rapidly — thousands of jobs lost

major_employers.name: New Bedford fishing fleet
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred boats, thousands of fishermen and shore workers

wage_benchmarks.occupation: Textile mill operative (where mills still ran)
wage_benchmarks.source: Georgianna, Daniel. The Strike of '28. Spinner Publications, 1993.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the {decade}s, the mills that had brought {ancestor_name}'s family to {county} County were closing — the looms were heading south where labor was cheaper and unions didn't exist, and the families that had built their lives around the factory whistle had to find something else or leave.
economic_eras.source: Wolfbein, Seymour Louis. The Decline of a Cotton Textile City. Columbia University Press, 1944. Georgianna, The Strike of '28.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Defense manufacturing (shipbuilding, electronics), suburban expansion, Route 128 technology corridor beginning, fishing sustaining, tourism growing
economic_eras.description: World War II revived the industrial economy. The Fore River Shipyard in Quincy built aircraft carriers and destroyers, employing 32,000 workers including women for the first time. Defense electronics firms established themselves along Route 128 in Norfolk County — the beginning of what would become America's first technology corridor. Postwar suburbanization transformed Norfolk County and the northern Plymouth County towns as the GI Bill enabled veterans to buy homes in former farmland. The mill cities (Fall River, Lawrence, New Bedford) never fully recovered — they attracted some light manufacturing but never regained their textile-era employment levels. Fishing remained a viable if declining industry in Gloucester, New Bedford, and Provincetown. Cape Cod's tourism economy expanded with automobile access.

county_specifics.note: Fore River Shipyard peaked at 32,000 workers. Route 128 corridor development began with Raytheon (Waltham, at the Norfolk border) and other defense electronics firms. Suburban development accelerated.
New Bedford fishing sustained the Portuguese community. Fall River attracted some garment manufacturing. Both cities remained economically depressed relative to their textile-era peak.
General Electric in Lynn (jet engines, 1940s) provided major defense employment. Lawrence remained economically struggling. Gloucester fishing fleet declined but survived.
Cranberry industry modernized. South Shore towns (Hingham, Cohasset, Scituate) became commuter suburbs. Brockton shoe manufacturing continued at reduced levels.
Boston's economy shifted toward education, healthcare, and financial services. The Charlestown Navy Yard and South Boston drydocks employed thousands during the war.
Tourism became the dominant industry. Cape Cod National Seashore established (1961). Year-round population grew modestly; seasonal population exploded.

key_infrastructure.name: Fore River Shipyard WWII expansion
key_infrastructure.impact: Built 5 aircraft carriers, 23 destroyers, and other vessels. Peak employment of 32,000, including women workers.

key_infrastructure.name: Route 128 construction
key_infrastructure.impact: Circumferential highway through Norfolk County suburbs. Technology companies located along the route, creating the first suburban technology corridor.

key_infrastructure.name: Massachusetts Turnpike
key_infrastructure.impact: East-west toll highway connecting Boston to western Massachusetts, improving access for suburban development.

major_employers.name: New Bedford fishing fleet
major_employers.employment_scale: Several hundred boats, primarily scalloping and groundfish

wage_benchmarks.source: Quincy Historical Society. Fore River Shipyard.
General Electric Company records; Bureau of Labor Statistics.
Saxenian, AnnaLee. Regional Advantage. Harvard University Press, 1994.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The war changed everything for {ancestor_name} — the shipyard at Quincy and the defense plants along Route 128 offered wages that the old mill economy never had, and the GI Bill offered a house in the suburbs that {pronoun_possessive} parents could never have imagined owning.
economic_eras.source: Saxenian, AnnaLee. Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1994.

economic_turning_points.impact: Jefferson's embargo on foreign trade crippled Salem's East India trade, Gloucester's fisheries, and Boston's merchant fleet. Maritime capital was redirected into manufacturing, seeding the industrial revolution. Salem never recovered its maritime supremacy.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The Embargo of {year} broke Salem's golden age in a single season — the ships that had carried {ancestor_name}'s prosperity to Canton and Sumatra sat at anchor while the counting houses on Derby Street went quiet.

economic_turning_points.impact: Over 100,000 Irish immigrants arrived in Boston between 1845 and 1855. The newcomers provided cheap labor for mills and factories, displacing Yankee mill girls and transforming the social order. Irish families also settled in mill towns (Lawrence, Fall River, Lowell) and quarry towns (Quincy).
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Boston from Ireland in {year}, the city was not welcoming — but the mills were hiring, and a wage that would not feed a family in Ireland could feed one in Massachusetts if everyone worked.
economic_turning_points.source: Handlin, Oscar. Boston's Immigrants. Harvard University Press, 1941.

economic_turning_points.event: Petroleum discovery begins the end of whaling
economic_turning_points.impact: Drake's petroleum strike in Titusville, Pennsylvania, provided a cheaper lighting fuel than whale oil. New Bedford's whaling fleet, which had peaked at over 300 vessels, declined steadily. The city pivoted to textile manufacturing.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The oil well in Pennsylvania in {year} killed the whale fishery that had made New Bedford the richest city per capita in America — {ancestor_name}'s family had to pivot from the harpoon to the loom within a single generation.

economic_turning_points.event: Bread and Roses strike in Lawrence
economic_turning_points.impact: Twenty-five thousand textile workers struck for ten weeks after a pay cut. The strike united immigrant workers across 25 nationalities and became a landmark in American labor history. The workers won — wages were restored and overtime pay was established.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: When the mills cut {ancestor_name}'s pay by thirty-two cents a week in January 1912, {pronoun_subject} walked out with twenty-five thousand other workers into the Lawrence winter — and stayed out for ten weeks until the company gave the money back.
economic_turning_points.source: Watson, Bruce. Bread and Roses. Viking, 2005.

economic_turning_points.impact: New England textile companies began relocating to the Carolinas and Georgia for cheaper labor and lower costs. Fall River and Lawrence lost thousands of jobs over two decades. The industrial economy that had sustained three generations of immigrant families collapsed.
economic_turning_points.narrative_hook: The mills didn't close all at once — they moved south one at a time, taking the looms and leaving the buildings. By {year}, {ancestor_name} could see the empty windows in the mills along {river_name} and know that the work was not coming back.
economic_turning_points.source: Wolfbein, The Decline of a Cotton Textile City.

narrative_hooks: In {county} County in the {decade}s, the economy ran on {commodity} — and {ancestor_name}'s family livelihood depended on whether the market, the catch, or the factory whistle held steady.

When {ancestor_name} arrived in {county} from {origin_county} in {year}, the work that drew {pronoun_object} was {industry} — an industry that would sustain {pronoun_possessive} family for a generation and then disappear.
{ancestor_name} knew the rhythm of {industry} work — the factory whistle at dawn, the clatter of {machines}, and the weekly pay envelope that meant the difference between eating and going hungry.
The harbor at {port} was where {ancestor_name}'s world connected to the wider economy — the ships that carried {cargo} outbound and brought {imports} home.

By the {decade}s, the {industry} that had brought {ancestor_name}'s family to {county} was dying, and the choice was the same one every generation had faced: adapt, move, or watch the wages disappear.

The GI Bill changed everything for {ancestor_name}'s generation — the veteran who had grown up in a {city} tenement could now buy a house on a tree-lined street in {suburb} and drive to the defense plant on Route 128.
source_references: Vickers, Daniel. Farmers and Fishermen: Two Centuries of Work in Essex County, Massachusetts, 1630-1850. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1994.
Dolin, Eric Jay. Leviathan: The History of Whaling in America. New York: W.W. Norton, 2007.
Dublin, Thomas. Women at Work: The Transformation of Work and Community in Lowell, Massachusetts, 1826-1860. New York: Columbia University Press, 1979.
Watson, Bruce. Bread and Roses: Mills, Migrants, and the Struggle for the American Dream. New York: Viking, 2005.
Silvia, Philip T. Victorian Vistas: Fall River, 1865-1885. Fall River: R.E. Smith Printing, 1987.
Wolfbein, Seymour Louis. The Decline of a Cotton Textile City. New York: Columbia University Press, 1944.
Handlin, Oscar. Boston's Immigrants, 1790-1880. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1941.
Cumbler, John T. Reasonable Use: The People, the Environment, and the State, New England 1790-1930. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2001.
Saxenian, AnnaLee. Regional Advantage: Culture and Competition in Silicon Valley and Route 128. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1994.
Georgianna, Daniel. The Strike of '28. New Bedford: Spinner Publications, 1993.
